strategy: PyMuPDF text + table extraction -> agentic chunking via Gemini

headings: TRAINING PROGRAMMING
CASE STUDIES

pages.text:
2

At this point you have learned everything you need to know to design optimal training
programs. Anything in this document is mostly practical and you could skip this
document entirely in principle. Designing an optimal program is a bit like a puzzle,
except that there isn't just a single right way to allocate the pieces. Often, there are
many different ways to structure a program and they will be similarly effective. As long
as your program fits the optimal training principles, you're all good. With that in mind,
here's a general framework of how to go about creating a training program.
1. Determine the total weekly training frequency. If the trainee is not averse to
training, this is generally equal to the highest number of sessions they're
consistently willing to go to the gym. Example, if someone's willing to go to the
gym 6x per week, the total training frequency will be 6x.
2. Determine the optimal weekly training frequency per muscle group for each
muscle. Example: A guy has his upper body as the priority, which is developed
at a serious intermediate level, so it probably needs to be trained at least 3x per
week for optimal progress, whereas the lower body is at an earlier intermediate
stage and only needs to be trained at least 2x per week.
3. Create the program split.
In the above example, you could train the upper body every other day and the
lower body twice a week. You could split the upper body in 2 workouts that you
alternate between with the pecs, front and lateral delts and triceps in one of them
and the biceps, lats, traps and rear delts in the other. On 4 of those days you
then add lower body workouts, one session for the calves and hamstrings and
one session for the erector spinae, quads and glutes.
Categorizations like push/pull and upper/lower are convenient here sometimes,
but don't get stuck in one-size-fits-all models like that. There are tons of options
on how to structure a training program: whatever fits the optimal training
frequency and volume is best. Note also that the training frequency per muscle
group is a minimum, not a rigid target. It's thus generally fine if some muscles are
trained more often than others.
headings: 1. Determine the total weekly training frequency. If the trainee is not averse to
2. Determine the optimal weekly training frequency per muscle group for each
muscle. Example: A guy has his upper body as the priority, which is developed
3. Create the program split.

pages.text:
3

4. Determine the optimal weekly total work set training volume for each
muscle. In the above example, it may be 15 sets for the upper body and 12 sets
for the lower body. That means each lower body muscle should do on average 6
sets per each workout; 5 sets per workout for the upper body muscles.
If you're going to implement training to failure or advanced training techniques,
take this into account and lower the volume for the affected muscles
correspondingly.
5. Select your exercises, their training intensities and progression models
based on i.a. the available equipment and their synergy, ensuring you hit the
minimum optimal exercise variety for each muscle group. For example, you need
to ensure you hit the hamstrings with a knee flexion and a hip extension exercise,
such as glute-ham raises at 70% and pull-throughs at 80% of 1RM. Since you
have 3 days on which you train the pecs, you could select either 2 or 3 different
pec exercises. If you select 2, you'll train each exercise 1.5x per week, which is
close to ideal. However, it's also fine to select 3 and train each exercise once per
week if you want higher variety for some reason, such as injury prevention. In
this case, you need to select 3 complementary exercises with different training
intensities. You would not want to select the decline, incline and flat bench press
all at 80% of 1RM, as they're too similar, especially in terms of injury risk.
Instead, go with something like Bayesian flys at 65%, flat bench presses at 80%
and suspended push-ups and 77.5% (an intensity in the middle of the other 2).
6. Allocate the exercises over the program split, paying attention to the
principles of exercise ordering to maximize total repetition volume. Set volume
can generally be split equally across your exercises. In the above example, that
would be 12 / 2 / 2 = 3 sets per exercise for the hamstrings and 15 / 3 / 1 = 5 sets
for the pecs.
7. Look back over the program and apply good ol' common sense. Most
programs require some ad hoc decision making. For example, maybe the gym
logistics mean you can't separate delt and pec exercises very well in the program
headings: 4. Determine the optimal weekly total work set training volume for each
muscle. In the above example, it may be 15 sets for the upper body and 12 sets
5. Select your exercises, their training intensities and progression models
6. Allocate the exercises over the program split, paying attention to the
7. Look back over the program and apply good ol’ common sense. Most

pages.text:
4

and they end up being trained in a fatigued state every session. Then add some
more sets to them to make up for the lower than planned volume.

The steps to creating an optimal training program.

➢ Bonus lecture [optional]
Reflecting on mechanisms of muscle growth

Determine total weekly training frequency
Determine training frequency per muscle group to create the training split
Determine work sets per muscle group
Select exercises and their training intensities and progression models per muscle group
Allocate the exercises over the training split
Do practicality check of the program
headings: ➢ Bonus lecture [optional]

pages.text:
5

Now we'll show some case studies as examples of what good programs may end up
looking like. Note that without the context of the email correspondence and without
being able to see exactly when what was changed in the programs, it is important to
focus on the general principles, not on trying to replicate these programs to the letter.

• Case study: Average Joe program design
This is a straightforward case study of the program design process for a typical PT
client: an intermediate level office worker that 'just wants to look good naked'.

• Case study: Novice vegan
This is a fictional case study created by the Bayesian team to illustrate the full decision-
making process of creating a program from scratch.

• Case study: Completely untrained individual
This is another fictional case study created by the Bayesian team to illustrate the full
decision-making process of creating a program from scratch.

• Case study: female Powerlifter training in a home gym [intake form]
This was one of Menno's female powerlifting clients tentatively preparing for a future
competition while cutting down to a more competitive weight class. She was training
daily.
Also, here's a more general case study on exercise selection in a home gym with
only free-weights

• Case study: Men's Physique competitor shoulder rehab [intake form]
headings: • Case study: Average Joe program design
• Case study: Novice vegan
• Case study: Completely untrained individual
• Case study: female Powerlifter training in a home gym [intake form]
Also, here’s a more general case study on exercise selection in a home gym with
only free-weights
• Case study: Men’s Physique competitor shoulder rehab [intake form]

pages.text:
6

This was another one of Menno's clients, a Men's Physique competitor far out from
competition in the off-season. The focus here was on rehabbing his shoulder and
maintaining with only 3 workouts a week. Notice the prioritization of muscle groups by
Men's Physique standards and the exercise selection

• Case study: booty building [intake form]
This was one of Menno's female clients with the almost sole focus being to build, as she
put it, massive glutes. Menno set up her program with instructions to follow the program
on her own, so they started with undulating periodization right from the start. She was
training daily.

• Case study: difficult progression
Here's the progression track of one of Menno's clients preparing for his first Physique
show. High stress, mediocre to poor sleep quality, moderate genetic potential, could
only train 4x a week and he sometimes had to train in the morning, so this is a good
example of poor progression with plenty of modifications. Menno highlighted and
commented on the most important changes in his progression plan so you can see how
we dealt with stagnation and how everything worked.

The notation is [weight] x [reps in first set] x [total number of sets]. This is
Autoregulatory Volume Training where you only log the first set and on the other sets go
by feel, focusing on explosiveness and good technique (but still 1 rep to failure) instead
of on achieving X reps.

Note that he often did not follow the progression guidelines as planned and e.g. stuck
with the same weight repeatedly after stalling.
headings: • Case study: booty building [intake form]
• Case study: difficult progression

pages.text:
7

• Case study: moving a national level powerlifting champion up a weight
class
This is the exact progression track Menno used to get one of his Powerlifting clients up
a weight class after he became national powerlifting champion in -83 kg. There was a
reason for the low amount of calf work, but Menno's doesn't remember (part of it was
limited equipment availability).

• Case study: IFBB Pro Bikini competitor
Below is a case study of an elite level bi-daily program for a woman. It's one of the
programs Menno created for Nina Ross that resulted in several international titles and
an IFBB Pro card. Notice how high the volume is when all volume influencing variables
are ideal and how the volume and frequency differ drastically per body part to get the
desired Bikini physique for her: glutes > quads / shoulders > hams > calves > rest. The
format in the periodization column is [reps on first set] x [sets] with Autoregulatory
Volume Training.
headings: • Case study: moving a national level powerlifting champion up a weight
class
• Case study: IFBB Pro Bikini competitor

• Case study: no gym, no weights, no problem
Here's a case study of an intermediate level client that started backpack-traveling the
world. He only occasionally had access to a gym, so Menno created his program almost
exclusively with bodyweight work, a TRX suspender and 2 light elastic training bands.
As you can see, with some creativity in terms of exercise selection and the application
of progressive overload, this can get you very far and he gained an excellent amount of
lean body mass during the coaching period.

• Case study: Block periodization and mixing different goals
You can consider this a bonus case study, as Powerlifting and Olympic Weightlifting
aren't core contents of the Bayesian PT Course, but it provides some insight in how to
deal with the common problem of clients wanting to achieve various, often interfering
goals all at once. As a coach you have to learn to really dig into the motivation of your
clients to see which goals are most important to them.

TRAINING PROGRAMMING
CASE STUDIES


--- Page 2 ---
2

At this point you have learned everything you need to know to design optimal training

document entirely in principle. Designing an optimal program is a bit like a puzzle,
except that there isn't just a single right way to allocate the pieces. Often, there are

• Case study: Average Joe program design
